flashcards.front: What is the Viewing Pipeline in computer graphics?
flashcards.back: The sequence of operations that transforms world coordinates to screen coordinates: Modeling transformation → Viewing transformation → Projection → Window-to-Viewport transformation → Device transformation.

flashcards.front: Define Window and Viewport in computer graphics.
flashcards.back: Window is a rectangular region in world coordinates defining what part of the scene to display. Viewport is a rectangular region in device/screen coordinates where the window is mapped.

flashcards.front: What is the UVN camera model?
flashcards.back: A camera model using three mutually perpendicular vectors: U (right vector), V (up vector), and N (view plane normal). The camera position defines the viewing coordinate system.

flashcards.front: What is the purpose of clipping in viewing?
flashcards.back: Clipping removes portions of objects that lie outside the view volume (window/frustum), ensuring only visible portions of the scene are rendered to improve efficiency and correctness.
A parametric line clipping algorithm that uses 4-bit region codes to quickly determine if a line is completely visible, completely invisible, or requires clipping against one or more boundary edges.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between Orthographic and Perspective projection?
flashcards.back: Orthographic: parallel projection lines, maintains relative proportions, no depth perception. Perspective: projection lines converge at a center of projection, creates depth perception with foreshortening.
A device-independent coordinate system with x and y ranging from -1 to +1 (or 0 to 1), used as an intermediate step between world coordinates and actual device coordinates.

flashcards.front: What is the viewing transformation matrix?
flashcards.back: A 4×4 transformation matrix that converts world coordinates to viewing (camera) coordinates by translating the camera to origin and aligning axes with the UVN system.
A pyramidal volume (for perspective) or parallelepiped (for orthographic) that defines the 3D region visible to the camera, bounded by near and far clipping planes.
The ratio of viewport width to height (W/H). It ensures objects are not distorted when displayed on screens with different proportions.

flashcards.front: What is the purpose of the projection transformation?
flashcards.back: Projects 3D viewing coordinates onto a 2D view plane. It converts the view volume to canonical form (unit cube) for subsequent clipping and rasterization.
A visibility determination technique that discards polygons facing away from the camera based on the dot product of surface normal and view direction.

flashcards.front: What is the View Volume in computer graphics?
flashcards.back: The 3D region in world space that is potentially visible from the camera position. For perspective projection it's a truncated pyramid; for orthographic it's a parallelepiped.
Maps points from the window (world coordinates) to the viewport (screen coordinates) using scaling and translation: sx = (xw - wxmin)/(wxmax - wxmin), sy similar formula.
A 2D surface (usually perpendicular to the view direction) onto which 3D objects are projected. Its position and orientation are defined by the camera parameters.
